why: AI makes interface rewrites and local reshaping cheaper, but ownership seams, coordination cost, and org coupling still dominate.

decision: Data ownership and source of truth
why: The expensive part is not code rewrite, it is semantic authority, migration, reconciliation, and customer/business correctness.
Adapters and contract migrations get easier, but partner/client coordination and compatibility windows still cost real time.
AI helps refactor implementations, but it does not make semantic mistakes in correctness, ordering, or money movement much safer.
Optimization and redesign get easier to attempt and revisit, though production bottlenecks and load behavior still matter.
Faster change velocity makes detection, attribution, rollback, and containment more central to business safety, even if instrumentation is cheaper.
The real cost is exposure, compliance, tenant isolation, and incident consequence; AI only modestly lowers implementation effort.
Progressive delivery, compatibility, migration safety, and rollback matter more when the system mutates much more often.
This becomes mostly regenerateable text unless it leaks into contracts, runtime behavior, or cross-team coupling.
Bad defaults may spread faster, but if they are internally visible and low-consequence, remediation is often mostly codegen plus cleanup.
As implementation gets cheaper, the higher-leverage question becomes which seams, capabilities, and constraints preserve business option value.
